stem: Let \( f_n(x) = \dfrac{[[a]]x}{x+n} \) for \( x \in [0, [[M]]] \); each \( f_n \) is increasing in \( x \) on this interval and \( f_n(x) \to 0 \) pointwise as \( n \to \infty \) for every fixed \( x \). Find \( \displaystyle\sup_{x \in [0,[[M]]]} f_n(x) \) when \( n = [[n]] \) (this sup-norm value is the quantity that must go to \( 0 \) for convergence to be uniform on \( [0,[[M]]] \)).
solution: Since \( f_n \) is increasing in \( x \) on \( [0,[[M]]] \), its supremum is attained at the right endpoint: \( \sup_{x\in[0,[[M]]]} f_n(x) = f_n([[M]]) = \dfrac{[[a]]\cdot [[M]]}{[[M]]+[[n]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the value of the coefficient \( a \) alone, ignoring the endpoint evaluation.
This swaps the roles of \( M \) and \( n \) in the endpoint value.
This omits the \( +n \) term in the denominator.
This drops the coefficient \( a \) from the correct endpoint value.
This is off by one from the correct supremum.
This is off by one from the correct supremum.

stem: Let \( f_n(x) = \dfrac{[[a]]\sin(nx)}{n} \) for \( x \in \mathbb{R} \), so \( f_n \to 0 \) pointwise (in fact for every \( x \)) as \( n \to \infty \). Since \( \sin \) attains every value in \( [-1,1] \), find \( \displaystyle\sup_{x \in \mathbb{R}} |f_n(x)| \) when \( n = [[n]] \) (this sup-norm value going to \( 0 \) is exactly what makes the convergence uniform on all of \( \mathbb{R} \)).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies instead of dividing by \( n \).
This uses \( n+1 \) in the denominator instead of \( n \).
This is off by one from the correct sup-norm.
This is off by one from the correct sup-norm.

stem: Let \( f_n(x) = [[a]]\,n\,x(1-x)^n \) for \( x \in [0,1] \); for every fixed \( x \in [0,1] \), \( f_n(x) \to 0 \) as \( n \to \infty \), but \( f_n \) is maximized at \( x = \dfrac{1}{n+1} \). Find \( \displaystyle\sup_{x \in [0,1]} f_n(x) \) when \( n = [[n]] \) (this value does NOT go to \( 0 \), which is exactly why the pointwise convergence to \( 0 \) here is not uniform).
solution: Evaluating at the maximizer \( x^* = \dfrac{1}{n+1} \): \( f_n(x^*) = [[a]]\cdot n \cdot \dfrac{1}{n+1}\left(\dfrac{n}{n+1}\right)^{n} = [[a]]\cdot\dfrac{n^{n+1}}{(n+1)^{n+1}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is just the coefficient \( a \), ignoring the maximizing computation entirely.
This resembles \( a/(n+1) \), missing the exponent on both \( n \) and \( n+1 \).
This multiplies \( a \) by \( n \) with no exponents at all.
This uses the wrong power (squared denominator only) instead of \( n+1 \) on both terms.
This is off by one from the correct supremum.
This is off by one from the correct supremum.
